meta.title: What's happening
meta.note: Curated cross-domain event log — the developments moving Mexico, each rewritten in plain English, dated, and linked to a first-party or record source. Auto-generated from the news wire on every refresh; raw or non-English items are quarantined, never published.

events.title: PRI proposes reform to curb excessive bank credit card fees
events.why: The party will push changes to the Credit Institutions Law aimed at limiting commissions and interest charges banks apply to credit cards.
events.background: Mexico's commercial banks lend little to the private sector relative to GDP, and consumer credit cards carry among the highest rates in that market, leaving households exposed to steep charges.
events.drivers: PRI coordinator Rubén Moreira cited credit products charging annual interest above 100% and total annual costs (CAT) above 200%, plus fees for not using a card, paying in cash or minor late payments.
events.next: The initiative will be discussed in the next legislative session.

events.title: ‘She was almost a Batman figure’: Mexico’s avenging Mother Courage
events.why: After her son disappeared, Isabel Miranda de Wallace swore she would bring the culprits to justice.

events.title: USTR prepares visit to Mexico as USMCA review continues
events.why: The U.S. Trade Representative acknowledged progress on so-called non-tariff barriers ahead of a planned visit to Mexico, part of the ongoing review of the USMCA trade pact.
events.background: USMCA governs Mexico-US-Canada trade and, since the US declined a 16-year extension at the July 2026 joint review, the pact now faces annual reviews through 2036. Economy Secretary Marcelo Ebrard's team has been negotiating with USTR for months on the underlying trade-barrier disputes.
events.drivers: Washington is sending negotiators for a third bilateral round, July 21-23 in Mexico City, to advance talks on steel and aluminum, autos, economic security, labor, agriculture and electronic payment services.
events.implications: Greer says the goal includes closing gaps that let third countries benefit improperly from the treaty, tying the review to non-tariff barriers already flagged in USTR's 2026 report.
events.next: The two delegations meet July 21-23 in Mexico City to continue the sector-by-sector talks.

events.title: BIS warns Banxico and other central banks on excessive debt purchases
events.why: The Bank for International Settlements flagged reputational risk for central banks, including Banxico, that intervene heavily in money markets by buying government debt.
events.background: Banxico sets its policy rate to hold inflation near its 3% target and, like other central banks, sometimes buys government debt in secondary markets to smooth market disruptions. Public debt as a share of GDP has been rising.
events.drivers: The BIS says elevated debt-to-GDP ratios in countries including Mexico complicate managing money supply and interest rates, which can force central banks to intervene more often to fix market dysfunctions.
events.implications: The BIS warns that repeated large-scale asset purchases or credit operations risk creating moral hazard for both financial markets and governments.

events.title: Nu eyes payroll banking to challenge BBVA and Santander in Mexico
events.why: Analysts say Nu Mexico's base of 15 million users gives it an opening to compete for payroll accounts, a segment long dominated by legacy banks.
events.background: Nu México just won approval to operate as a bank, entering a market where BBVA and other legacy banks dominate payroll accounts, the anchor product banks use to lock in other business.
events.implications: Winning payroll clients would strip traditional banks of a cheap financing source tied to their corporate payroll agreements.
events.drivers: Nu points to its 15 million Mexican users to challenge that dominance, and analysts note payroll portability is a worker's right, not the employer's, so better direct terms could shift accounts quickly.

events.title: Business council says USMCA's future hinges on 2026 or 2029 U.S. elections
events.why: CCE leader José Medina Mora made the comments ahead of a third round of negotiations between Mexico and the United States over the trade pact's review.
events.background: USMCA's joint review process, triggered after the U.S. declined a 16-year extension in July 2026, now requires annual reviews under Article 34.7, with the treaty otherwise running through 2036.
events.drivers: CCE president José Medina Mora says the treaty's direction still depends largely on Trump's own position, based on conversations with US agencies ahead of the third negotiating round.
events.next: Mexico and the U.S. begin a third round of USMCA negotiations.
events.implications: The private sector's own timeline shows the annual-review structure could leave USMCA's terms unsettled for years rather than resolved soon.

events.title: Finance ministry misses deadline to issue rules under anti-money-laundering reform
events.why: The 2025 reform was billed as the biggest update to Mexico's anti-laundering regime in over a decade, but Hacienda's delay in issuing implementing rules stalls its application.
events.background: The July 2025 reform to Mexico's anti-money-laundering law, LFPIORPI, took effect in March and was meant to tighten oversight of real estate, vehicle sales, jewelry and other 'vulnerable activities' through new beneficial-owner identification and cash-use rules.
events.drivers: Hacienda has not published the general provisions needed to operationalize several of the law's new obligations, including beneficial-owner identification, cash-use limits and new SAT reporting schemes.
events.implications: The regulatory vacuum leaves the vulnerable-activity sectors the reform targeted without the rules needed to comply in practice.

events.title: 'El Mayo' Zambada awaits life sentence in the United States
events.why: The former Sinaloa cartel leader, over 70, faces sentencing on charges of trafficking tons of drugs into the U.S., a case with weight in the bilateral security relationship.
events.background: Zambada co-founded the Sinaloa Cartel with Joaquín 'El Chapo' Guzmán in the late 1980s and was captured in an unusual arrest in July 2024 after decades evading US authorities.
events.next: His defense has asked that, if he receives a life sentence, he be held in a federal prison where he can get specialized medical care.

events.title: Baja California governor's US visa restoration draws scrutiny
events.why: Marina del Pilar Ávila regained her U.S. visa after showing willingness to cooperate with Washington, a contrast with the Sheinbaum government's stated defense of sovereignty.
events.background: Trump administration officials have pursued an assertive approach toward Mexico under the banner of fighting 'narcoterrorism,' prompting Sheinbaum's government to make sovereignty defense a top priority and to ask officials and legislators to avoid actions that concede to Washington.
events.drivers: The article contrasts Sheinbaum's sovereignty stance with a governor who has shown willingness to do whatever it takes to win U.S. favor in order to get her visa back.
events.implications: The governor's approach undercuts the unified sovereignty posture the federal government has tried to project toward Washington.

events.title: Mexico extends its venture capital lead over Brazil in Latin America
events.why: Mexican startups raised 944 million dollars in the second quarter, up 131 percent from a year earlier, pulling further ahead of Brazil as global venture firms increase their presence in the region.
events.background: Latin America's venture market has been split mainly between Mexico and Brazil, with global funds increasingly active in the region's fintech, logistics and transportation startups.
events.drivers: Investors including Founders Fund and Andreessen Horowitz led some of the quarter's largest deals, and Mexico-based companies took the region's three biggest fundraising deals in Q2.
events.implications: A continued boom in late-stage and growth funding lifted the whole region, with Latin American startups raising $1.36 billion in the quarter, up 47% year over year.

events.title: Antitrust fine against Liverpool and Gicsa heads to competition courts
events.why: The fine, notified in June and rooted in a 2020 investigation by the former competition regulator, will test how Mexico's competition tribunals handle enforcement cases from that era.
events.background: The National Antimonopoly Commission (CNA) succeeded Cofece as Mexico's competition regulator and inherited pending cases, including a 2020 probe into possible monopolistic practices in commercial real estate leasing.
events.drivers: The fine of more than 500 million pesos lets sanctioned firms, including Fibra Danhos and Grupo Acosta Verde, challenge the ruling before Mexico's two specialized competition courts.
events.implications: The case could test how far Cofece-era investigations reach in current tribunals.
events.next: The dispute could eventually reach Mexico's Supreme Court after the specialized courts rule.

events.title: Volvo, Honda and Toyota diverge on Mexico investment strategy
events.why: Volvo's bet on Mexico stands out against the different paths taken by other automakers, as manufacturers weigh where to place capacity amid shifting trade and tariff conditions.
events.background: Automakers have been recalibrating North American production amid shifting trade and tariff conditions under USMCA, weighing new Mexican capacity against existing U.S. plants.
events.drivers: Volvo's new Ciénega de Flores plant began trial runs in May, and CEO Martin Lundstedt told analysts output there will rise gradually through the second half of the year despite June delivery problems.
events.implications: Volvo will keep covering part of North American demand, including for its Mack Trucks unit, from its Virginia and Pennsylvania plants while the Nuevo León line ramps up.

events.title: Mexico clears environmental and security hurdles ahead of USMCA review round
events.why: Washington has acknowledged Mexican action on avocado deforestation, cross-border wastewater, and dual-use export controls, clearing the way for the July 21 talks in Mexico City.
events.drivers: Washington acknowledged concrete Mexican action on avocado deforestation, cross-border wastewater and dual-use export controls, moves Mexico brought into the round to clear ground before tougher fights.
events.implications: Clearing these issues sets up the harder negotiations on steel, autos and agriculture that negotiating teams will now tackle.
events.next: Negotiating teams will spend three days in Mexico City working through steel and related issues.
events.background: The USMCA Joint Review is the annual process the US, Mexico and Canada now use after Washington declined a 16-year automatic extension in July, with the treaty otherwise running through 2036.

events.title: El Mayo Zambada sentenced to life, ordered to forfeit $15 billion
events.why: A New York court sentenced the former Sinaloa Cartel leader after his guilty plea, in a case with continuing implications for US-Mexico security cooperation.
events.background: Zambada, arrested in a disputed 2024 operation, had pleaded guilty months earlier to decades of trafficking cocaine, heroin and fentanyl as a Sinaloa Cartel leader.
events.drivers: Judge Brian Cogan confirmed the life sentence for trafficking cocaine, heroin and, in recent years, fentanyl, following the earlier guilty plea.
events.implications: The sentence puts Zambada in the same position as Guzmán, both former Sinaloa Cartel leaders now imprisoned for life in the United States.

events.title: Hacienda's vehicle leasing tender faces collusion probe
events.why: The tender covers leasing up to 18,000 vehicles for the federal government, and authorities are examining possible collusive bids among contractors.
events.background: SHCP's tender covers vehicle leasing for federal agencies, a recurring type of public contract where bid patterns are checked for signs of coordination among bidders.
events.drivers: An analysis found Casanova Vallejo, Grupo Turbofin and Impulsa Tu Ganancia submitted bids with minimal differences among themselves, priced above the most competitive offers submitted by 11 total bidders.
events.implications: The pattern raises questions about whether the winning bid for a four-year, 90-plus-agency contract reflects genuine competition.
events.next: A decision on the tender is due within days.

events.title: EU accuses CEMEX and rivals of coordinating construction chemical prices
events.why: The European Commission says CEMEX and other firms coordinated price increases in France, Germany, and Spain between 2021 and 2022, a case that could bring fines.
events.background: CEMEX is a Mexican multinational cement producer with major operations in Europe, now facing a European Commission antitrust inquiry into construction chemical additives.
events.drivers: The Commission's preliminary view is that CEMEX and other manufacturers and industry associations coordinated price increases on additives used in cement, concrete and mortar in three markets.
events.implications: The formal notification of the Commission's preliminary view exposes CEMEX to potential fines over the 2021-2022 conduct.

events.title: Sheinbaum defends US relationship but says Mexico won't 'bow its head'
events.why: The president criticized the Trump administration over treatment of migrants and interference in Mexican sovereignty while affirming the diplomatic relationship.
events.background: Mexico's president serves one six-year term and has no reelection; Sheinbaum took office in October 2024 amid recurring friction with Washington over migration, security and sovereignty.
events.drivers: Sheinbaum responded to unauthorized US agency operations inside Mexico and to mistreatment of Mexican migrants reported by her government.
events.implications: The two countries are in a prolonged period of bilateral tension, driven especially by Trump's threats against Mexico under the pretext of fighting "narcoterrorism."

events.title: Mexico's grain imports hit a decade-high as import dependence grows
events.why: Grain imports, including corn, reached 23.9 million tonnes in the first half of 2026, the highest level since 2015.
events.background: Corn is one of Mexico's most consumed foods, and grain purchases include soybeans, soybean meal and canola alongside it.
events.drivers: GCMA data show the increase was driven by corn imports up 8.6%, soybeans up 9.8%, soybean meal up 34.1% and canola up 10.6% versus the same period in 2025.
events.implications: The $7,581 million import bill is the second-highest for any first half on record, trailing only 2022, when international food prices spiked after the Russia-Ukraine war began.

events.title: Mexico receives first locomotives for Maya Train freight service
events.why: The 12 locomotives mark a milestone toward the freight service's planned January 2027 start.
events.background: The Maya Train is a rail project in southeastern Mexico; its freight service is planned to begin commercial operations in January 2027.
events.drivers: The arrival of the first 12 line-haul locomotives marks the shift from civil works to operational preparation for the freight line.
events.next: Sheinbaum said the locomotives are already being used to transport construction materials while the government reaffirms the plan for commercial freight operations.

events.title: Mexico to regulate mobile phone use in schools nationwide
events.why: The federal government will present legislation following nationwide consultations with educators, parents, and experts.
events.drivers: The federal government held nationwide consultations with educators, parents and experts before drafting the measure.
events.background: The proposal responds to concerns raised nationally about how minors use mobile devices in educational settings.

events.title: USTR pushes for stricter USMCA auto rules of origin ahead of review
events.why: Jamieson Greer proposed tightening rules of origin for the auto industry as trade talks with Mexico continue this week, a change that would affect how much Mexican-made vehicle content qualifies for tariff-free treatment.
events.background: Under Article 34.7, the US, Mexico and Canada now hold annual reviews of USMCA after the US declined a 16-year extension in July, with the treaty running through 2036 unless withdrawn or extended; auto rules of origin determine how much Mexican-made vehicle content qualifies for tariff-free treatment.
events.drivers: Trump, Sheinbaum and Canada's Mark Carney met cordially at the World Cup final even as the US pushed to tighten auto rules of origin ahead of the bilateral round.
events.next: The third round of bilateral negotiations on the joint USMCA review runs from Tuesday July 21 to Friday July 24 in Mexico City.
events.implications: Coverage cited in the article notes the annual reviews have already dealt a psychological blow to private investment in Mexico.

events.title: Trump approves 50% tariff on Canada amid USMCA negotiations
events.why: The tariff targets autos, dairy and alcohol over what Trump calls discriminatory treatment of US products, adding pressure to the parallel USMCA review that includes Mexico.
events.background: USMCA trade now proceeds under annual reviews rather than a long extension, after the US declined to renew the pact for 16 years on July 1.
events.drivers: Trump says the tariffs offset what he calls discriminatory treatment of US products by Canada, covering motor vehicle materials, dairy and alcohol.
events.implications: The tariffs were announced the same day the second round of the trilateral USMCA review began.
events.next: The Trump administration said the tariffs take effect in 30 days.

events.title: Wage gap between US and Mexico becomes a USMCA review issue
events.why: A Baker Institute study finds US workers earn nearly 10 times more than their Mexican counterparts, adding pressure on Mexico to narrow the gap as the 2026 USMCA review proceeds.
events.background: USMCA labor provisions and wage standards have drawn scrutiny as Mexico's informality rate, at 55.2% of workers, keeps millions outside formal pay and bargaining structures.
events.drivers: A Baker Institute study attributes the gap, sharpest in automotive manufacturing, to limited union bargaining power rather than differences in skill.
events.implications: The wage disparity intersects with rising labor informality, which reached 55.2% in May 2026, complicating efforts to close the gap.

events.title: Pemex reports oil leak and fire at Dos Bocas refinery
events.why: The company said standard safety procedures contained the fire with no injuries reported, at a refinery central to the government's fuel self-sufficiency plans.
events.background: Dos Bocas is the refinery central to the government's plan to make Mexico self-sufficient in gasoline and diesel, but it has run over budget and below capacity since opening.
events.drivers: A leak in an oil loading pump ignited a fire and black smoke at the refinery, which Pemex's safety procedures and firefighting staff extinguished.

events.title: Chinese-brand vehicle sales in Mexico rise nearly 30% despite tariffs
events.why: Sales grew in the first half of the year even as Mexico maintains tariffs on Chinese vehicles, according to AMDA data cited by Reuters.
events.background: Mexico imports far more from China than it exports there, and the government has used tariffs to slow Chinese vehicle imports while protecting the domestic auto market.
events.drivers: A 50% tariff took effect in January specifically to curb Asian automotive imports, yet Chinese brands still expanded sales through the first half of the year.
events.implications: Chinese automakers captured 17% of Mexico's new vehicle market in the period, up from 14% a year earlier.

events.title: NetShape opens $13.6 million auto parts plant in Querétaro
events.why: The Shape Corp subsidiary's new facility at the FINSA industrial park adds manufacturing capacity in Mexico's automotive supply chain.
events.background: Querétaro sits in Mexico's industrial corridor where auto-parts suppliers have expanded, one of the few areas where nearshoring investment is visible in new construction and hiring.
events.drivers: Shape Corp's Mexican subsidiary NetShape invested to scale up production capabilities at the FINSA Industrial Park facility.
events.implications: The project is projected to create more than 260 new manufacturing jobs in the region.

events.title: Alsea's profit drops 52% as Starbucks Mexico sales weaken
events.why: Net sales and EBITDA fell in the second quarter compared with last year, reflecting weaker consumer spending pressuring the company's Starbucks Mexico operations.
events.background: Alsea operates Starbucks and Vips restaurants in Mexico, businesses sensitive to household spending in an economy where private consumption has been sluggish.
events.drivers: Alsea's CEO cited a 'challenging consumption environment' in Mexico, with Starbucks facing particularly difficult conditions during the quarter.
events.implications: Same-store sales growth slowed from 4.9% a year earlier to 2.6%, and results missed analyst forecasts that had expected a smaller profit decline of 26%.

events.title: USMCA Review Keeps Industrial Real Estate in Cautious Recovery
events.why: Gross absorption of industrial space rose 14% year-on-year in the first half of 2026, but lingering trade uncertainty from the USMCA review is keeping developers and export-oriented manufacturers on the sidelines, according to Datoz and CBRE.

events.title: Former governor Ruffo's fuel-smuggling hearing drags on with army links alleged
events.why: The ongoing case against ex-Baja California governor Ruffo includes a businessman accused of running a separate fuel-contraband network involving Defense Ministry personnel, tying the trial to broader concerns about military involvement in huachicol fiscal.
events.background: Huachicol fiscal is the tax-fraud scheme of smuggling fuel while declaring only part of it, and Ruffo was the first opposition politician to unseat the PRI as Baja California governor.
events.drivers: The case also implicates businessman Riestra Fernández, accused of running a separate contraband network involving Defense Ministry personnel, while political rhetoric for and against Ruffo's detention keeps building.
events.implications: The article notes the case matters to both sides: PAN feels bound to defend a historic figure, while Morena, facing its own governors accused of organized-crime ties, gets a chance to shift attention.

events.title: Mexico files criminal complaints against ICE agents over migrant deaths
events.why: Mexico's government is testing a criminal law route after 17 Mexicans died in ICE custody, raising the diplomatic temperature with Washington over immigration enforcement.
events.background: ICE detention centers are privately run facilities that hold migrants for US federal enforcement operations, a system that has expanded sharply under the current administration.
events.drivers: An Associated Press count puts deaths tied to the enforcement drive at 62, including 17 Mexicans, after agents shot two people and a Venezuelan detainee died of a heart attack during a transfer in Georgia.
events.implications: The complaints raise the diplomatic tone between Mexico and Washington over how migrants are treated in US custody.

events.title: Court orders pretrial detention for former Baja California governor Ruffo in fuel theft case
events.why: A judge ordered former PAN governor Ernesto Ruffo and seven others held in the Altiplano maximum-security prison after a lengthy initial hearing on charges tied to a fiscal huachicol (fuel theft) scheme, a case that touches both organized crime and high-level political corruption.
events.background: Ernesto Ruffo was Baja California's governor and the first candidate to defeat the PRI in a gubernatorial race, making him a historic figure for the PAN. Huachicol fiscal refers to schemes that evade fuel taxes rather than physically stealing pipeline fuel, and such cases often surface amid Mexico's broader organized-crime and corruption investigations.
events.drivers: After more than a day of hearings, a judge ordered Ruffo and seven co-defendants held for possible organized crime and contraband offenses, sending them to the Altiplano maximum-security prison.
events.implications: The case has become political ammunition for both the ruling party and the opposition, who dispute whether the arrest is a distraction from accusations of ties between Morena and organized crime or a legitimate judicial process.

events.title: Teamsters push Trump to impose steep tariffs on Mexican beer imports
events.why: The union's request adds pressure on trade policy ahead of the USMCA review, targeting a major Mexican export category to the U.S. market.
events.background: Mexican beer exports, led by brands like Modelo and Corona, are a major category in U.S.-Mexico trade currently under scrutiny as part of the USMCA review process.
events.drivers: The Teamsters argue Mexico's government-backed investment incentives, lower labor costs and export-focused policies have let imported beer capture a growing share of the U.S. market, and want production shifted to American breweries.
events.implications: The request adds another sector for the USTR to weigh as it assesses whether other countries' policies have created unfair manufacturing advantages.
events.next: The USTR continues assessing whether such policies have fueled unfair manufacturing advantages.

events.title: Mexico says 83% of gas stations now comply with diesel price cap
events.why: The federal government's voluntary ceiling of 27 pesos per liter for diesel has been adopted by most stations, a step meant to contain fuel costs and inflation pressure.
events.background: The diesel price cap is a voluntary agreement backed by the Energy and Finance ministries, Pemex and Profeco, aimed at containing fuel costs.
events.implications: Baja California has the most non-compliant stations, with 231 selling above the cap, showing the effort remains geographically uneven.
events.drivers: After a July 12 announcement, 435 more stations joined the agreement, raising participation as authorities push toward full compliance.

events.title: Sheinbaum faces scrutiny over Morena governors' alleged cartel ties
events.why: Reports point to governors in Baja California, Sinaloa, Tamaulipas and Sonora facing US scrutiny over suspected links to organized crime, testing the government's response as it downplays the allegations.
events.background: Sheinbaum has built her US relationship on a pragmatic, low-friction approach even as Mexico's federal judiciary and states operate under new electoral rules following the 2024 judicial reform.
events.drivers: Leaked audio and reports have widened US scrutiny to governors in Baja California, Sinaloa, Tamaulipas and Sonora, with Baja California's Marina del Pilar Ávila the latest named.
events.implications: The escalating US allegations test the sovereignty-based defense Sheinbaum has used for four months, even as she has managed the Trump relationship carefully.

events.title: Former Baja California governor Ruffo detained in cartel-linked case
events.why: The arrest of the first opposition governor in recent memory lands amid US scrutiny of Morena governors' alleged cartel ties, reshaping the political fight over organized crime.
events.background: Ernesto Ruffo, Mexico's first opposition governor after defeating the PRI in Baja California in 1989, is now central to a fuel-smuggling probe the government says has run over a year.
events.drivers: Ruffo's arrest gives Morena political relief during one of its most difficult weeks handling the Baja California and Sinaloa cartel-tie scandals.
events.implications: The case reframes the corruption narrative toward opposition figures rather than Morena governors, and marks the first time in Sheinbaum's term an opposition figure has been detained.

events.title: Chinese-made heavy trucks in Mexico complicate bid to ease Trump tariffs
events.why: A growing fleet of China-built trucks operating in Mexico gives Washington another argument to maintain tariff pressure, tying trade relief to scrutiny of Chinese content in Mexico's supply chains.
events.background: USMCA's annual review process now scrutinizes whether Mexico's export platform, especially trucking, is becoming a route for Chinese manufacturers into North America.
events.drivers: Chinese truck import value into Mexico rose more than sevenfold over six years and the number of Chinese truck firms operating there reached about 23, alarming US officials and Mexican manufacturers.
events.implications: The trend complicates Mexican negotiators' push for tariff relief on an integrated export industry, since it undercuts Mexico's case for alignment with the US against China.

events.title: Supreme Court justices' salaries cut for second consecutive year
events.why: The reduction in pay and benefits for Mexico's top judges follows a broader push to trim public-sector compensation, extending austerity measures into the judiciary.
events.background: The 2024 judicial reform replaced Supreme Court appointment with election, and pay for the new justices is constitutionally capped so no official earns more than the president.
events.drivers: Official pay tables in the 2026 federal budget cut justices' compensation roughly 35% from the previous court, which had earned about 206,000 pesos net monthly plus benefits.
events.implications: Even after the cut, to up to 191,657 pesos, Supreme Court salaries remain among the highest in the federal public sector.

events.title: Mexican health authorities investigate cyclosporiasis outbreak tied to Taylor Farms
events.why: Cofepris and Senasica are investigating after US authorities reported 1,644 cases linked to the supplier, prompting preventive measures in Mexico amid cross-border food safety concerns.
events.background: Cofepris and Senasica are Mexico's health and agricultural safety regulators, tasked with inspecting food exporters and responding to cross-border outbreaks.
events.drivers: US authorities reported 1,644 cyclosporiasis cases tied to shredded iceberg lettuce, prompting Taylor Farms to recall products sourced from Mexico.
events.implications: Mexican authorities describe their response as preventive only, with inspections and traceability analysis but no confirmed cause yet.
events.next: A joint technical group has been formed to investigate and adopt further preventive measures.

events.title: Six journalists killed in Mexico since January amid press attacks
events.why: Ongoing attacks on media outlets and reporters across the country have left six journalists dead this year, with arrests made in only one case, raising questions about the state's protection of the press.
events.background: Mexico's press operates amid recurring violence against reporters, with INEGI data showing most crimes nationally go unreported or uninvestigated.
events.drivers: Six journalists have been killed since January, the latest Josué Martínez Contreras, shot in Puebla while his 13-year-old son was present.
events.implications: Arrests have been made in only one of the six killings, underscoring gaps in protection and prosecution.

events.title: Mexico cuts World Cup economic impact forecast
events.why: Deloitte lowered its estimate of the tournament's economic impact on Mexico by 7%, citing weaker than expected performance in restaurants, hotels and entertainment venues.
events.background: The 2026 World Cup was co-hosted by the US, Canada and Mexico, with 48 teams and 104 matches, and organizers had promoted it as the most commercial edition yet.
events.drivers: Deloitte lowered its forecast citing weaker than expected activity in restaurants, hotels and entertainment venues during the tournament.
events.next: Official final figures for the tournament's economic impact are still pending.

events.title: Mexico returns letters to ICE, testing Sheinbaum's approach to Washington
events.why: The Trump administration accuses Mexico of overstepping its sovereignty by protesting migrant deaths in detention centers, a dispute that tests how far Sheinbaum will push diplomatic channels versus public confrontation.
events.background: Mexico's protest letters over migrant deaths in ICE custody were sent through its embassy, the normal diplomatic channel with Washington.
events.drivers: The US State Department, through official Michael Kozak, returned the letters to Mexico's new ambassador Roberto Lazzeri and told Mexico to use diplomatic channels rather than public protest.
events.implications: The episode tests whether Sheinbaum's government can keep pressing the ICE deaths issue without straining the channel Washington insists on using.

events.title: Former Baja California governor Ernesto Ruffo arrested on organized crime charges
events.why: Prosecutors said the arrest, tied to a fuel-theft investigation into his company, followed a high-complexity probe; a business partner was also detained.
events.background: Huachicol fiscal, or fuel-tax smuggling, is a recurring target of Mexican prosecutors investigating customs and tax fraud tied to fuel imports.
events.drivers: The FGR describes a high-complexity investigation into Ingemar, a company Ruffo founded that he has said only handled customs paperwork, leading to his arrest along with business partner Ricardo Thompson Navarro.
events.implications: A former governor now faces organized crime and tax-fuel-smuggling charges, tying a prominent political figure to an alleged contraband network.
events.next: Ruffo has been transferred, handcuffed, to FGR facilities in Tijuana.

events.title: Honda halts sales of its Mexico-made electric SUV in the US
events.why: Honda will pull the Prologue EV from the US market and shift toward hybrids, citing weak electric vehicle demand, a setback for Mexico's EV manufacturing push.
events.background: The Honda Prologue is an electric SUV built in Mexico and co-developed with General Motors, part of Mexico's push to attract EV manufacturing investment.
events.drivers: Honda cited weak US demand for the decision, telling dealers production will end this year as the company shifts toward gasoline hybrids and combustion models.
events.implications: The move follows other automakers scaling back EV production in response to soft demand in the US market.
events.next: Sales will stop starting with the 2026 model year as Honda ends Prologue production.

events.title: Pemex halts oil exports to Cuba for first time in 33 years
events.why: The state oil company stops shipments after Trump administration warnings, a move that signals Mexico's caution in handling energy ties with Havana under US pressure.
events.background: Pemex is Mexico's state oil company, financially tied to the federal government, and Cuba has long received Mexican crude as one of its main fuel sources.
events.drivers: Exports fell to zero dollars in May, a decline that began in January amid US pressure on Mexico to avoid supporting Havana, with President Trump threatening intervention against Díaz-Canel.
events.next: Despite Trump's threats, Sheinbaum said in late June that Mexico will keep sending petroleum to Cuba.
events.implications: Exports to Cuba fell to zero in May, the first such stoppage since records began in 1993, showing Mexico's caution in balancing US pressure against its own foreign-policy stance.

events.title: Slim's Grupo Carso buys 30% stake in Mexican oil field from TotalEnergies
events.why: Carso will take a stake in Block 30, home to the KAN field operated by Harbour Energy, extending the conglomerate's push into Mexico's oil sector as the government seeks private investment in energy.
events.background: Pemex's crude output has fallen to its lowest level in four decades, pushing the state company to seek private partners like Grupo Carso, which already works as a Pemex service contractor on fields such as Ixachi.
events.drivers: Carso is buying TotalEnergies' 30% stake in Block 30, home to the KAN field, alongside operator Harbour Energy, which holds the remaining 70%.
events.next: The deal's closing depends on obtaining the corresponding government authorizations.
events.implications: Slim has said Pemex's low production is Mexico's core problem, even after declining in May to pursue further business directly with the state oil company.

events.title: Mexico issues travel advisory for the US over intestinal infection outbreak
events.why: The advisory responds to a rise in cyclosporiasis cases across several US states, an unusual reversal of the typical direction of health warnings between the two countries.
events.background: Sinave is Mexico's national epidemiological surveillance system, which typically issues warnings about outbreaks abroad affecting Mexican travelers.
events.implications: The advisory marks an unusual reversal, with Mexico now cautioning its citizens about conditions in the United States rather than the other way around.
events.drivers: Cyclosporiasis cases have risen in Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio and West Virginia, with more than 400 cases accumulated in the outbreak.

events.title: BlackRock says annual USMCA reviews will bring uncertainty to Mexico investment
events.why: BlackRock does not expect Mexico to lose its sovereign investment-grade rating, but flags the treaty's annual review mechanism as a recurring source of uncertainty for investors in the country.
events.background: USMCA's annual review mechanism, triggered after the US declined a 16-year extension, has replaced the certainty of a long guaranteed term with yearly reassessment through 2036.
events.drivers: BlackRock's José Luis Ortega said the US refusal to renew removed the chance for greater investment certainty this year, leaving long-term capital unwilling to commit aggressively.
events.implications: BlackRock does not expect Mexico to lose investment-grade status, but says persistent uncertainty means high economic growth from investment is unlikely to materialize.

events.title: Finance ministry accelerates cuts to productive investment to control fiscal deficit
events.why: Hacienda is cutting spending on productive investment ahead of the second half of the year in an effort to contain the fiscal deficit, according to the report.
events.background: Hacienda, Mexico's finance ministry, is trying to narrow the fiscal deficit partly by leaning on Pemex, the state oil company that already strains public finances through the support it requires.
events.drivers: Despite SAT collecting more than ever from tax credits this year, physical investment in Pemex fell to 77,188 million pesos in the first five months, its lowest level since 2008.
events.implications: Capital for new Pemex machinery fell 45.7% in real terms over five months, its steepest historical drop for the period, even as social development spending continues rising.

events.title: Regulator CNBV fines BanFeliz amid possible change of control
events.why: The banking regulator issued four fines of 481,100 pesos each against the digital bank, its first sanctions, as the institution appears to be undergoing an ownership change.
events.background: The CNBV is Mexico's banking regulator; BanFeliz took control of Banco Forjadores in 2024, inheriting an institution that had already accumulated regulatory sanctions.
events.drivers: The fines stem from monitoring, procedural and client-verification deficiencies dated to 2022, before BanFeliz's takeover, and mark the first sanctions published under its administration.
events.implications: None of the infractions were classified as serious, and all remain open to challenge, which limits their weight amid the reported change of control.
events.next: The sanctions are subject to appeal by the bank.

events.title: Mexico's imports from Asia grow 42% despite tariffs
events.why: Purchases from Taiwan, China and other Asian countries rose 42.4% through May while trade with the Americas grew only 4.5%, suggesting tariff pressure has not curbed Asian sourcing.
events.background: Mexico applies tariffs on 11 countries without a trade agreement, including China, India, South Korea, Vietnam, Thailand and Taiwan.
events.drivers: Imports from Asia rose from $108,971 million to $155,226 million between January and May, led by a 233.76% jump in purchases from Taiwan, while imports from the Americas grew only 4.49%.
events.implications: Asia's share of Mexico's total imports climbed to 50%, suggesting the tariffs have not curbed the shift toward Asian sourcing.

events.title: Toyota's Tacoma displaces Mexico's top vehicle export to the US
events.why: The Tacoma became the single most-exported vehicle from Mexico to the United States in the first half of 2026, according to trade data cited in the report.
events.background: The Tacoma is built at Toyota's Tijuana plant (TMMBC) and has been Mexico's top vehicle export to the United States; Mexico exported 1.28 million vehicles to the US in the first half of 2026, down 3.6% annually.
events.drivers: Toyota is moving Tacoma production to its San Antonio, Texas plant, a shift it says will be gradual over about four years.
events.implications: Mexico stands to lose its leading vehicle-export line, since Tacoma and Tacoma HEV units made up 124,871 and 15,665 of Toyota's 140,536 exports in the period.
events.next: Toyota expects the San Antonio plant's output to grow by about 150,000 units, close to the volume currently exported from Mexico, as the transfer proceeds over roughly four years.

events.title: Foxconn and Invetec deepen Taiwan's tech investment in Mexico
events.why: Taiwanese manufacturers including Foxconn and Invetec are expanding investment in Mexico, much of it tied to AI hardware, reflecting the growing trade and technology relationship between the two economies.
events.background: Taiwanese manufacturers have expanded in Mexico as AI hardware demand grows, building on an already deepening trade relationship between the two economies.
events.implications: The expansions position Mexico more deeply in AI hardware supply chains and point to further Taiwanese investment interest.
events.next: Other Taiwanese companies are reported to be considering investments in the country.
events.drivers: Inventec is investing $450 million to expand its Ciudad Juárez data-center design operations, while Foxconn has committed $136.7 million this year for AI server production, on top of $168 million announced in August 2025.

events.title: Grupo Carso buys TotalEnergies' 30% stake in a Gulf of Mexico oil block
events.why: Carlos Slim's conglomerate expands into offshore oil exploration, adding private capital to a sector still dominated by Pemex.
events.background: Grupo Carso is Carlos Slim's conglomerate, which has been expanding into Gulf of Mexico oil and gas blocks as Pemex, facing declining crude output, seeks private partners.
events.drivers: Carso is buying TotalEnergies' 30% stake in Block 30 through a subsidiary, with Harbour Energy retaining 70% and continuing as operator.
events.next: The deal's closing is subject to regulatory approval.
events.implications: Slim has said his companies will avoid new joint ventures with Pemex, so the purchase extends his push into oil and gas segments outside state control rather than partnering with the state company.

events.title: Banxico deputy governor says inflation risks remain tilted to the upside
events.why: Jonathan Heath said the balance of risks has not turned favorable despite June's slowdown, a signal the central bank may hold off on further rate cuts.
events.background: Banxico targets 3% inflation, give or take one percentage point, and Jonathan Heath sits on its governing board that sets the policy rate.
events.implications: His comment that the balance of risks is not yet favorable suggests Banxico may proceed cautiously on further rate moves.
events.drivers: Heath noted June's inflation reading, at 3.37% annually, understated how favorable the second half of the month actually was, but he questioned whether the improvement will last through the rest of the year.
events.next: Heath said the balance of risks is not yet favorable, signaling continued close monitoring of incoming data before any shift in stance.

events.title: Honda to end electric vehicle production in Coahuila
events.why: Weak EV demand is prompting Honda to halt its electric model in Mexico, following a similar pullback by Toyota and raising questions about EV manufacturing plans in the country's auto export belt.
events.background: Honda's Prologue is an electric SUV developed with General Motors and built at the Ramos Arizpe plant in Coahuila; it launched for the 2026 model year.
events.implications: The decision raises questions about the future of electric-vehicle manufacturing plans at Mexican plants built for the US market.
events.next: Production at Ramos Arizpe will stop by the end of the year, ending Prologue sales with the 2026 model.
events.drivers: Weak US demand for electric vehicles is pushing Honda to end Prologue sales after the 2026 model, shifting its US strategy toward hybrids and combustion-engine vehicles that show more market activity.

events.title: Sheinbaum says courts will be impartial in fuel-theft case against ex-governor Ruffo
events.why: The president rejected claims of political persecution, saying the investigation into former Baja California governor Ernesto Ruffo began a year ago and more arrests are expected.
events.background: Huachicol fiscal is fuel-tax contraband, and Sheinbaum's daily press conference is where the government routinely responds to political controversies like Ruffo's arrest.
events.drivers: Sheinbaum said the investigation had been running for a year before eight arrest warrants were issued, rejecting the idea that it targets the opposition politically.
events.implications: The case tests whether Mexico's newly elected judiciary can rule independently in a politically charged prosecution involving the opposition.
events.next: Security chief García Harfuch said more arrest warrants remain to be issued and additional people are under investigation.

events.title: Officials detail huachicol network linked to former governor Ruffo
events.why: Security chief García Harfuch said the fuel-theft ring declared only 10% of its rail tanker capacity, part of an investigation open more than a year that has produced eight arrests so far.
events.background: The huachicol fiscal case against Ruffo stems from a fuel-contraband network investigated by the Fiscalía Especializada en Materia de Delincuencia Organizada.
events.drivers: Investigators traced the network after seizing 33 rail tankers and found the organization declared only about 10% of each tanker's real capacity to evade fiscal controls.
events.implications: Authorities consider it among the largest fuel-contraband structures they have identified.
events.next: The investigation, open more than a year, continues.

events.title: US-Iran conflict threatens a fifth of global oil supply, pressuring gasoline prices
events.why: Shippers are avoiding the Strait of Hormuz as the standoff escalates, raising logistics costs that could feed through to fuel prices in Mexico.
events.background: Mexico imports large volumes of US natural gas for its power system, tying its electricity supply to developments in global energy markets.
events.implications: The added shipping costs threaten to feed through to fuel prices just as international markets were beginning to stabilize after an earlier round of increases.
events.drivers: Renewed US-Iran conflict is forcing oil companies and shipping operators to avoid the strait, adding up to 14 days and higher costs by rerouting around Africa's Cape of Good Hope.

events.title: US health officials trace cyclosporiasis outbreak lettuce to Mexico
events.why: Taco Bell recalled lettuce from US restaurants after authorities linked a parasite outbreak to a Mexican supplier, prompting new scrutiny of cross-border food safety.
events.background: Cyclosporiasis is a parasitic intestinal illness that causes vomiting, fatigue and diarrhea, and can spread through contaminated produce.
events.drivers: A joint FDA and CDC investigation traced the sole supplier of shredded iceberg lettuce used at affected Taco Bell locations to Mexico, after outbreaks appeared in Indiana, Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio and West Virginia.
events.implications: US media have named Taylor Farms as the company involved, though neither authorities nor Taco Bell have confirmed it.
events.next: Taco Bell said the affected ingredient from its supplier will be pulled indefinitely.

events.title: Apollo readies up to $20 billion to finance Mexican infrastructure
events.why: The firm is in talks to fund infrastructure projects, particularly in energy and transport, pointing to continued foreign capital interest in Mexico despite trade tensions.
events.background: Commercial-bank credit to Mexico's private sector is only about 35.5% of GDP, a gap foreign private-credit funds have been moving to fill, particularly for infrastructure.
events.drivers: Apollo is in private talks to fund infrastructure projects in energy and transport, a move the article says aligns with President Sheinbaum's strategy of attracting private capital for strategic infrastructure.
events.implications: Projects tied to power generation, renewables and transmission modernization have started gaining momentum under the current administration even as some large-scale projects face delays.
